topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the stark lettering of the genetic report mocking me. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to keep from dropping it. It was impossible. After years of carefully constructed narratives about where I came from, this cold, undeniable data shattered everything. I stared at the names listed—names that weren't mine, names that belonged to a family I’d never known. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound of pure, corrosive disbelief. I pushed the paper away with unnecessary force, the sound of the tearing paper loud in the sudden silence of the apartment. Every muscle in my jaw was tight, a knot of tension pulling painfully beneath my skin. I needed air, needed to escape the suffocating reality that my entire foundation had just crumbled into dust.

[story 2]

I sat bolt upright in the stiff armchair, the air in the room suddenly too thin to breathe. The confirmation stared back at me on the screen, a cruel, undeniable truth laid bare in blue text. My breath hitched, a sharp, ragged intake of air that burned in my chest. I felt a sudden, intense heat rising in my face, a flush that started in my neck and spread rapidly. I slammed my fist onto the wooden armrest, the impact jarring my wrist. "No," I whispered, the sound raspy and hollow. The silence that followed was thick with suppressed fury. I stood up, pacing the small living room like a caged animal, my steps heavy and deliberate. Each movement was sharp, fueled by a deep, churning resentment that threatened to spill over into something destructive. I wanted to scream until my vocal cords gave out, to shake the very foundations of the house.

[story 3]

The envelope felt heavy, dense with unspoken accusations. I read the summary twice, my eyes scanning the genetic markers as if searching for a hidden flaw, a mistake in the science. When I finally looked up, the room seemed too bright, too loud. A deep tremor ran through my hands, making the paper flutter precariously. I slowly folded the document, the motion agonizingly deliberate, as if trying to physically contain the shock surging through my core. I stood by the window, watching the street below, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. A bitter taste coated my tongue. I clenched my fists so tightly that my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. The sheer unfairness of it settled over me like a physical weight, pressing down on my chest until it felt impossible to draw a full breath.
